goal_13.config.title: Vulnerability and adaptation to climate-related hazards
goal_13.config.abstract: Extreme weather events, such as floods, droughts, tornadoes, and heatwaves  occur more often and are stronger due to climate change and impact more than half the world’s population. A third of those affected are also highly vulnerable and require adaptation to a changing climate.
goal_13.content.#1.text: Chapter will focus on targets 1 and 2:
goal_13.content.#2.text: 13.1 Strengthen resilience and adaptive capacity to climate-related hazards and natural disasters in all countries
goal_13.content.#3.text: 13.2 Integrate climate change measures into national policies, strategies and planning.
goal_13.content.#5.text: Several indicators provide evidence of a changing climate. Globally, temperatures have been steadily increasing compared to the average for the period of 1850-1900.
goal_13.content.#6.title: Global mean temperature 1850-2024
goal_13.content.#6.subtitle: Difference from 1850-1900 average
goal_13.content.#8.title: Top 50 countries with most hot days in 2020s
goal_13.content.#8.subtitle: Number of hot days per year, 2020 average
goal_13.content.#8.scenes.#0.text: As the average temperature increases, more extreme weather events happen more frequently. This includes ‘hot days’ defined as days with temperature in excess of XX degrees.
goal_13.content.#8.scenes.#1.text: While you might expect these countries to be on a top-50 list by the number of hot days, the number of days has increased quite dramatically in these countries compared to the average in the period YYYY-ZZZZ.
goal_13.content.#10.text: The impacts of a changing climate not only affects average and extreme temperatures. Changing precipitation patterns are also affected, and irregular precipitation, droughts and floods are impacting agricultural production possibilities as well as the health and safety of individuals.
goal_13.content.#13.title: Global mean sea level change
goal_13.content.#13.subtitle: Millimeters
goal_13.content.#13.visdescription: As temperatures increase, sea levels are increasing as well. Since the early 1990s, sea levels have increased by approximately 100 millimeters. And they are expected to rise by XX millimeters by [2050/2100].
goal_13.content.#15.text: Effects of Climate Change on People, Communities, and Infrastructure
goal_13.content.#16.text: Highlight key climate-induced disasters (floods, droughts, storms, heatwaves).
goal_13.content.#17.text: Provide examples of recent disasters impacting human lives and infrastructure.
goal_13.content.#19.text: People at high-risk of climate impact
goal_13.content.#20.text: 1.2 billion people are at high risk of climate impact. Based on exposure (4 climate hazards) and vulnerability.
goal_13.content.#23.text: Visualize the following 4 climate hazards using the following data on a global map and introduce the new World Bank Corporate Scorecard Indicator: "People at High Risk to Climate-related Hazards." Starting by presenting population density, hazard exposure and vulnerability at a global scale one by one, and finally overlaying to show the ‘population at risk’ at a global scale.
goal_13.content.#24.title: Climate related disasters (floods, droughts, cyclones, and heatwaves)
goal_13.content.#24.subtitle: Vis subtitle
goal_13.content.#24.scenes.#0.text: There are four categories of climate-related disasters that the World Bank analyses in terms of exposure to climate hazards
goal_13.content.#25.text: The poorest are the most vulnerable
goal_13.content.#27.title: Vis title
goal_13.content.#27.subtitle: Vis subtitle
goal_13.content.#29.text: [emphasis: What follows are implementation notes and not part of chapter:]
goal_13.content.#31.text: Floods (pluvial, fluvial and coastal), file: flood_PFUC_3arcsec.tif
goal_13.content.#35.text: Use global spatial (mercator) maps showing each of the four climate hazards.
goal_13.content.#36.text: Overlay all four climate hazards on a global map
goal_13.content.#37.text: Add population density to show where people are exposed to climate hazards, files GHSL_degurban2_2020_res3arcsec.tif +  GHSL_degurban2_2020_res3arcsec.tif.aux.xml and GHS-POP2020_SDSN-RAI_3arcsec.tif]
goal_13.content.#40.text: [Visualization showing 100 (or more?) people representing the global population:
goal_13.content.#41.text: Showing the share of global population exposed to each of the four hazards (and bring in urban/rural) - data in
goal_13.content.#45.text: [tile map of word with share of people exposed for each country (click on a tile and the 4 different risk values show up?)]
goal_13.content.#47.text: Being exposed to climate hazards does not tell the full story of climate hazards. Whether people are affected by these events depends on their level of vulnerability.
goal_13.content.#48.text: [Use previous visualization showing global population but this time showing the share being vulnerable]
goal_13.content.#49.text: How do we determine who is vulnerable?
goal_13.content.#50.text: [scrollie of tile map showing the three categories of vulnerability
goal_13.content.#56.text: [Map of 100/200 people representing global population overlapping exposure and vulnerability - overlapping the maps above]
goal_13.content.#58.text: Combining the two dimensions of exposure and vulnerability to show people at risk
goal_13.content.#59.text: Allow users to choose individual countries
goal_13.content.#60.text: On the card that pops up for each country, present a drop down menu choosing dimensions of exposure and vulnerability
goal_13.content.#62.text: Select two countries with similar hazard exposure, but differing vulnerability and thus differing numbers of people at risk. [SUGGESTIONS: Jamaica vs. Dominican Republic. Thailand vs. Philippines. For extreme differences, look at Korea vs. Lao to visualize differences between a high-income and lower-middle income country from the same region.]
goal_13.content.#63.text: Explain differences in adaptive capacities and implications for risk.
goal_13.content.#65.text: Highlight spatial variation within a single country
goal_13.content.#66.text: Showcase regional disparities in exposure, vulnerability and risk.
goal_13.content.#68.text: Present data illustrating changes in climate risk between 2010 and 2021 from key indicator.
goal_13.content.#69.text: Contrast two countries with different rates of progress:
goal_13.content.#70.text: Country showing significant progress in reducing climate risk.
goal_13.content.#71.text: Country with minimal or negative progress.
goal_13.content.#72.text: Discuss factors influencing these trends (e.g., policy implementation, investment in infrastructure, institutional capacity).
goal_13.content.#74.text: What can governments do to improve climate resilience? Introduce the Adaptation and Resilience Readiness Assessment Tool:
goal_13.content.#75.text: Brief explanation of the tool’s purpose and approach.
goal_13.content.#76.text: Highlight the tool's relevance in prioritizing resilience actions.
goal_13.content.#77.text: Apply this tool to the two previously selected countries [Present interactive graphics that peel back layers of the country-level readiness scores, illustrating their subscores and indicators, with explanations of why interesting.]
goal_13.content.#78.text: Demonstrate how these detailed readiness indicators directly correlate with observed differences in vulnerability, or not.
goal_13.content.#79.text: Provide practical examples of actions prioritized or implemented, explicitly tied to specific readiness indicators and scores.
goal_13.references.keyOfReference: Here goes the content of the reference, which can include links like this. Add a reference in the text with [reference: keyOfReference]
